LO 39F · UNE COM Anatomy

Muscles of Facial Expression

Describe & demonstrate the muscles of facial expression — their origins, insertions, actions, innervation, and clinical significance.

01 / Overview

§ General Principles

The facial muscles (muscles of facial expression) reside in the subcutaneous tissue of the anterior and posterior scalp, face, and neck. They move the skin to change facial expressions and convey mood. Most attach to bone or fascia at one end and pull the skin at the other.

All muscles of facial expression develop from mesoderm in the second pharyngeal arch. A subcutaneous muscular sheet forms during embryonic development, spreading over the neck and face, carrying branches of the nerve of the arch — the facial nerve (CN VII) — with it.

Key Principles

  • All muscles of facial expression are innervated by CN VII (Facial Nerve)
  • They are sphincters and dilators of the facial orifices (mouth, eyes, nose)
  • They are derived from the 2nd pharyngeal arch mesoderm
  • They reside in subcutaneous tissue (not deep fascia)
  • Platysma and facial muscles are often fused due to common embryological origin
⚡
Motor Innervation: All muscles of facial expression are supplied by the facial nerve (CN VII) via its posterior auricular branch or via the temporal, zygomatic, buccal, marginal mandibular, or cervical branches of the parotid plexus.
🧬
Embryology: The muscular sheet differentiates into muscles that surround the facial orifices (mouth, eyes, nose), serving as sphincter and dilator mechanisms that produce facial expressions.
02 / Anatomy

§ Muscles of Scalp & Face

Fig 8.16A Anterior View
Anterior view showing the muscles of the scalp and face, including occipitofrontalis (frontal belly), orbicularis oculi, nasalis, zygomaticus major & minor, orbicularis oris, buccinator, and platysma.
Fig 8.16B–C Lateral Views
Lateral views showing the muscles and branches of CN VII (1 – posterior auricular, 2 – temporal, 3 – zygomatic, 4 – buccal, 5 – marginal mandibular, 6 – cervical). View C shows the deeper buccinator and pterygomandibular raphe.
Fig 8.17 Muscles of Facial Expression in Action
Muscles of facial expression demonstrated in action. These superficial sphincters and dilators of the orifices of the head are supplied by CN VII and produce the full range of facial expressions.
03 / Regional Groups

§ Muscles by Region

🧠 Scalp, Forehead & Eyebrows

The occipitofrontalis is a flat digastric muscle with occipital and frontal bellies sharing the epicranial aponeurosis. The frontal belly elevates the eyebrows and wrinkles the forehead (surprise). The occipital belly retracts the scalp. The corrugator supercilii draws the eyebrow medially creating vertical wrinkles above the nose (worry/concern).

Occipitofrontalis Corrugator supercilii Procerus

👁️ Orbital Opening

The orbicularis oculi closes the eyelids and wrinkles the forehead vertically. It has three parts: the palpebral part (gentle closure — blinking/sleep), the lacrimal part (aids tear drainage), and the orbital part (tight closure — winking/squinting). Fibers sweep in concentric circles around the orbital margin.

Orbicularis oculi — Palpebral Orbicularis oculi — Lacrimal Orbicularis oculi — Orbital

👄 Mouth, Lips & Cheeks

The orbicularis oris is the oral sphincter controlling entry/exit through the oral fissure — essential for speech. The buccinator keeps the cheek taut during chewing, presses cheek against molar teeth, and resists distension when blowing. Dilator muscles radiate like spokes from the lips. The modiolus is where ≤9 muscle fibers interlace at the mouth angle.

Orbicularis oris Buccinator Zygomaticus major Zygomaticus minor Levator labii sup. Levator anguli oris Risorius Depressor anguli oris Depressor labii inf. Mentalis

👃 Nose & Ears

The nasalis (alar part) flares the nostrils during anger or exertion. The procerus depresses the medial eyebrow and wrinkles the nose dorsum (disdain). Ear muscles are functionally unimportant in humans but important in animals that direct ears toward sound.

Nasalis (alar part) Procerus Auricularis (sup/ant/post)

🫁 Neck (Platysma)

The platysma is a broad, thin sheet in the subcutaneous tissue of the neck. Acting from its superior attachment, it tenses the skin producing vertical ridges (stress). Acting from its inferior attachment, it depresses the mandible and draws mouth corners down (grimace). The anterior borders of both sides decussate over the chin.

Platysma
Fig 8.18 Orbicularis Oculi — Parts & Actions
A. Parts of orbicularis oculi (orbital O and palpebral P). B. Palpebral part gently closes eyelids. C. Orbital part tightly closes the eyelids.

All innervated by CN VII

(frontal belly)		 Epicranial aponeurosis Skin & subcutaneous tissue of eyebrows and forehead Elevates eyebrows; wrinkles forehead skin; protracts scalp
Surprise / Curiosity		 Temporal
Occipitofrontalis
(occipital belly)		 Lateral ⅔ of superior nuchal line Epicranial aponeurosis Retracts scalp; increases effectiveness of frontal belly Post. Auricular
Orbicularis oculi Medial orbital margin; medial palpebral ligament; lacrimal bone Skin around orbit; superior & inferior tarsal plates Closes eyelids: palpebral part gently; orbital part tightly (winking) Temporal Zygomatic
Corrugator supercilii Medial end of superciliary arch Skin superior to middle of supra-orbital margin Draws eyebrow medially & inferiorly; vertical wrinkles above nose
Concern / Worry		 Temporal
Procerus + transverse nasalis Fascia over nasal bone & lateral nasal cartilage Skin of inferior forehead, between eyebrows Depresses medial eyebrow; wrinkles nose dorsum
Disdain / Dislike		 Buccal
Nasalis (alar) + Levator labii sup. alaeque nasi Frontal process of maxilla Major alar cartilage; lateral skin of upper lip Depresses ala laterally, dilating anterior nasal aperture ("flaring nostrils"); elevates upper lip
Anger / Exertion		 Buccal
Orbicularis oris Medial maxilla & mandible; deep surface of peri-oral skin; modiolus Mucous membrane of lips Tonus closes oral fissure; phasic contraction compresses & protrudes lips (kissing); resists distension (blowing) Buccal
Buccinator Mandible, alveolar process of maxilla, pterygomandibular raphe Angle of mouth (modiolus); orbicularis oris Presses cheek against molar teeth; keeps food between occlusal surfaces; resists distension (blowing) Buccal
Levator labii superioris Infra-orbital margin (maxilla) Skin of upper lip Elevates/everts upper lip; deepens nasolabial sulcus
Sadness		 Buccal
Zygomaticus minor Anterior aspect, zygomatic bone Skin of upper lip Elevates upper lip Buccal
Zygomaticus major Lateral aspect of zygomatic bone Angle of mouth (modiolus) Elevates labial commissure — bilaterally to smile; unilaterally to sneer
Happiness / Disdain		 Buccal
Levator anguli oris Infra-orbital maxilla (canine fossa) Angle of mouth (modiolus) Widens oral fissure (grinning/grimacing) Buccal
Risorius Parotid fascia & buccal skin (variable) Angle of mouth (modiolus) Depresses labial commissure bilaterally to frown
Sadness		 Buccal
Depressor anguli oris Anterolateral base of mandible Angle of mouth (modiolus) Depresses labial commissure (frowning) Marg. Mandib.
Depressor labii inferioris Platysma & anterolateral body of mandible Skin of lower lip Depresses/everts lower lip
Pouting / Sadness		 Marg. Mandib.
Mentalis Body of mandible (anterior to inferior incisors) Skin of chin (mentolabial sulcus) Elevates & protrudes lower lip; elevates chin skin
Doubt		 Marg. Mandib.
Platysma Subcutaneous tissue of infraclavicular & supraclavicular regions Base of mandible; skin of cheek & lower lip; modiolus; orbicularis oris Depresses mandible (against resistance); tenses skin of inferior face & neck
Tension / Stress		 Cervical
05 / Clinical & Functional Details

§ Functional Anatomy in Detail

🎺
Buccinator — "The Trumpeter Muscle": Named from Latin buccae (cheeks) — it compresses the cheeks during blowing. Works with orbicularis oris and the tongue to keep food between occlusal surfaces during mastication. Also resists forces generated by whistling and sucking. In the absence of orbicularis oris and buccinator tone, crooked or protrusive ("buck") teeth develop.
👁️
Orbicularis Oculi — Three Parts: (1) Palpebral part — gentle closure (blinking, sleep), keeps cornea from drying. (2) Lacrimal part — passes posterior to lacrimal sac, draws eyelids medially, aids tear drainage. (3) Orbital part — overlies orbital rim, tightly closes lids (winking, squinting) to protect against glare and dust. Contraction narrows the palpebral fissure laterally to medially.
🔀
The Modiolus: Lateral to the angles of the mouth (commissures), fibers of up to 9 facial muscles interlace in a highly variable, multiplanar formation called the modiolus — largely responsible for the occurrence of dimples.
⚡
CN VII Branches — "To Zanzibar By Motor Car": Temporal → Zygomatic → Buccal → Marginal mandibular → Cervical. These branches exit from the parotid gland to supply all muscles of facial expression.
